ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for butter crab; FOODON_03302051 (crab dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers cooked crustaceans broadly; no specific butter crab entry exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps specifically to butter crab as a prepared dish.

preparation_method: cooked with curry leaves and chili

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical individual serving of butter crab in a Singapore seafood restaurant is approximately 250 g, representing a shared portion of one whole crab with sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Butter Crab — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for Butter Crab (cr_id F03060003). All core macros and secondary nutrients sourced from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 11 g/100 g based on butter content proportion; HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this dish — see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for butter crab. GI estimated at 35 using mixed seafood dish with sauce as the closest analogous category from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Carbohydrate content is 9.8 g/100 g; GL per 250 g serving = (35 × 9.8 × 2.5) / 100 = 8.6. Low GI category assigned.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1037 mg/100 g — far exceeding the 600 mg/100 g threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit butter crab due to its extremely high sodium content of 1037 mg/100 g; if consumed, restrict to a very small portion (under 50 g) and avoid additional sauces or condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High saturated fat (~11 g/100 g) may worsen insulin resistance despite low sugar and moderate carbohydrate
type2_diabetes.guidance: Butter crab has a low glycaemic index and only 9.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making it acceptable for blood glucose control in moderate portions, but the high saturated fat from butter may impair insulin sensitivity over time — request reduced-butter preparation and limit portion to 150 g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~11 g/100 g), trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g), and sodium (1037 mg/100 g) all elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Butter crab is high in saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium — all established cardiovascular risk factors — and should be consumed only occasionally and in small portions by individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease; request reduced-butter preparation and avoid high-sodium accompaniments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat ~11 g/100 g and trans fat ~0.1 g/100 g exceed RA thresholds; sodium 1037 mg/100 g worsens tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid butter crab due to its high saturated fat, trace trans fat, and very high sodium, all of which promote systemic inflammation and joint swelling; chili (a nightshade) may additionally trigger joint flares in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Butter Crab is a rich Singaporean seafood dish in which whole crab — typically mud crab or flower crab — is wok-cooked in a generous quantity of butter together with curry leaves, fresh chili, garlic, evaporated milk, and egg yolk to form a thick, aromatic, golden sauce. The dish is a staple of Singapore's zi char (煮炒) seafood restaurant culture and is closely associated with the mamak and local Chinese seafood cooking traditions. The texture of the crab meat is firm and sweet, while the sauce is intensely buttery, mildly spiced, and slightly creamy. Nutritionally, butter crab is energy-dense at 299 kcal per 100 g, with high fat (21.9 g/100 g, predominantly saturated from butter at approximately 11 g/100 g), moderate protein (15.7 g/100 g), and moderate carbohydrate (9.8 g/100 g). Sodium is very high at 1037 mg/100 g, making this dish clinically unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or fluid retention. Calcium is notably high at 295 mg/100 g, contributed by the crab shell and evaporated milk. The glycaemic index is estimated as low (~35), making blood glucose impact modest in small portions. The dish is not suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease or rheumatoid arthritis due to its saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium load. It is best consumed occasionally and in moderate portions as part of a shared meal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in butter crab?
Is butter crab high in sodium?
What is butter crab made of?
Is butter crab suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore database (cr_id F03060003) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from butter content proportion and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central butter values; trans fat is a minor estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from a peer-reviewed source. All other values are HPB-sourced.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for butter crab not separately reported in HPB Foodo database as of 2026. potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish and are not available from a secondary approved source for this specific preparation. saturated_fat_g estimated at 11.0 g/100 g based on butter proportion in the dish cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for butter (SR Legacy 01001); this is an approximation. trans_fat_g estimated at 0.1 g/100 g from butter-derived trans fat (vaccenic acid) consistent with USDA butter data; this is a minor estimate. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed as negligible per HPB source; curry leaves and chili contribute trace fibre absorbed into the sauce. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) for mixed seafood dish with sauce; no direct GI measurement for butter crab exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Halal status marked false as butter crab typically uses butter which may not be halal-certified and the dish is associated with non-halal zi char establishments; individual restaurant certification should be verified.
